patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v4 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M
Re: [PATCH v4 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch limits the number of iterations for shrink_delalloc in Btrfs to prevent potential deadlocks or system stalls, and is part of a larger series that has been reverted due to unforeseen issues.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo proposed a patch that adds a condition to set nr_pages to 1 when it is zero in the shrink_delalloc function, citing that this solves the dead looping kworker on arm64 issue but does not fix the test case itself which still runs indefinitely. They believe the old 3 loops limit is what makes the difference and consider their solution 'ugly' but effective.
review_comments.raw_body:
I go with the following diff:

diff --git a/fs/btrfs/space-info.c b/fs/btrfs/space-info.c
index e931deb3d013..2c5214b24239 100644
--- a/fs/btrfs/space-info.c
+++ b/fs/btrfs/space-info.c
@@ -770,6 +770,9 @@ static void shrink_delalloc(struct btrfs_space_info 
*space_info,
                 u64 items = calc_reclaim_items_nr(fs_info, 
iter_reclaim) * 2;
                 int async_pages;

+               if (nr_pages == 0)
+                       nr_pages = 1;
+
                 btrfs_start_delalloc_roots(fs_info, nr_pages, true);

                 /*


It solves the dead looping kworker on arm64, now the it's several 
different kworkers taking around 5~15% along with 027 itself taking CPU 
time.

But unfortunately the test case itself still seems not to end any time soon.

I believe the old 3 loops limit is really what makes the difference.
It's ugly, but at least it seems to work.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that their original patch contained an unbounded loop and a min() bug, which caused the system to get stuck waiting on a commit. They expressed regret for including such code and implied they will rework the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for re-testing. Glad to hear that at least fixes the dead loop.

Honestly I should have known better than to include an unbounded loop,
that was stupid. I even kind of thought it was dumb while doing it but
convinced myself it "must make progress one extent at a time" or
whatever.. Obviously overlooked the min() bug too.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that in extreme ENOSPC situations, the delalloc shrinking loop can get stuck due to a semi-deadlock between metadata reservation and folio writeback, particularly when large folios are involved, and suggested adding a proper way to break out of the loop when no progress is made.
review_comments.raw_body:
Not an expert on the space reclaim part, but as far as I understand, 
there can be some cases where the only dirty folios that can be flushed 
is the folios that are being written back.

E.g. we have a large folio (256KiB) dirty, and that's the only dirty 
data folio in the whole fs.

Then at delalloc time, we are reserving metadata, and we are waiting for 
the ticket to shrink delalloc.

Although the ticket is trying to flush delalloc, the 256KiB dirty folio 
is already under write back, thus it will reclaim no space.
Thus the loop never progresses.

This will cause a semi-deadlock, as we want to reserve metadata to do 
the folio writeback, but the metadata reserving is trying to flush the 
same folio to progress.

And the recent huge folio feature is making the problem more obvious, as 
the folios grow larger, we have higher chance to hit such situation.


With that said, it means delalloc shrinking is never guaranteed to make 
any progress under extreme ENOSPC situation.
Thus a proper way to break out the loop is highly recommended, when 
there is no progress to be done.

Hopes this educated guess would help to explain the problem.

Thanks,
Qu

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v4 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch limits the number of iterations for shrink_delalloc in Btrfs to prevent potential deadlocks or system stalls, and is part of a larger series that has been reverted due to unforeseen issues.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that on their arm64 machine, the first three patches are not the root cause of the issue, but the last patch is still causing excessive CPU usage and consistently hangs the system within a certain time limit.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that on arm64 systems during g/027 test, the CPU usage is consistently high (~100%) on a kworker process, which was used as the bisecting criteria due to its reliability and speed.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that the kworker process is experiencing 100% CPU usage due to what appears to be a deadlock loop, indicating a significant performance issue.

Qu Wenruo reported that the patch series causes a hang on their system, and provided a call trace showing a deadlock between btrfs_async_reclaim_metadata_space and _raw_spin_unlock_irqrestore. They suspect that excessive space reclaim/pinning reclaim is choking the workload due to excessive reservation.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed understanding for the challenges of ENOSPC in btrfs, but prefers to revert the last commit to continue regular testing.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sorry, at least on my arm64 machine, the first 3 patches are not the 
root problem.

In fact on v7.0-rc7, I can still hit generic/224 hang, aka kernel 
detects 120s time out for hung processes, and my VM is configured to 
reset after such detection.

I'm going to slightly loose the hung task detection time (120s->150s) 
and check if it's just too slow in this particular case.


Although the last patch is still causing excessive CPU usage here, and 
very reliably.

---

At least we got something that both of us can reproduce.

Another thing is, for g/027 on arm64 I'm also actively monitoring the 
CPU usage through top.

Have you experienced very high (~100%) CPU usage on a kworker during g/027?

That's the most reliably symptom on my arm64 systems, and that's the 
criteria I used to bisect, as it takes less than 5 seconds to determine 
if it's good or not.

---

At least to me, the biggest problem is the 100% CPU usage of the 
kworker, which indicates a pretty bad dead looping.

---

Unfortunately it didn't even finish after 15m here.
And there is the dmesg with time stamps, the calltrace is triggered by
"echo l > /proc/sysrq-trigger".

[   30.140269] run fstests generic/027 at 2026-04-25 07:19:32

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that the system is stuck waiting on a commit, but has not been able to reproduce the symptom and is curious if Sun's proposed fix resolves the issue.

Author acknowledged that the patch series breaks tests, agreed to revert it until they understand what's happening and plan a fix.
review_comments.raw_body:
No :(
As far as I can tell the system is stuck waiting on a commit. I'll keep
trying to repro your symptom.

I'm curious if it goes away for you with Sun's proposed fix, something
like setting pages to at least 1 after those to min() operations.

Such a patch had no impact on the behavior of either of my systems,
though.

---

Totally agreed. I'm going to revert the whole series till I understand
what happened here.

Thanks,
Boris

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v4 4/4] btrfs: cap shrink_delalloc iterations to 128M
Re: [RFC PATCH v6 3/5] mm: Hot page tracking and promotion - pghot
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Hot page tracking and promotion patch series, focusing on mm subsystem improvements.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised concern over potential over-optimization and suggested keeping the patch simple, avoiding re-building DAMON functionality. Also pointed out a minor issue with kmigrated_run() optimization.

review_comments.author: Donet Tom
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged Gregory's concerns and apologized for the lengthy response. Asked a follow-up question about kmigrated_run() optimization.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Gregory

On 4/24/26 6:51 PM, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Fri, Apr 24, 2026 at 06:27:02PM +0530, Donet Tom wrote:
>>> +
>>> +	/*
>>> +	 * Record only accesses from lower tiers.
>>> +	 */
>>> +	if (node_is_toptier(pfn_to_nid(pfn)))
>>> +		return 0;
>>
>> Just a thought\u2014could we check this at the beginning of the function, before
>> the switch case?
>>
>>
> Can you please trim responses to omit un-related code? Not all of us use
> mail clients that auto-collapse quoted regions and it takes a lot of
> scrolling to reach here n_n;;;


Apologies for that, and thanks for pointing it out. I\u2019ll keep the 
responses concise and include only the relevant parts going forward.

>
>>> +static void kmigrated_do_work(pg_data_t *pgdat)
>>> +{
>>> +	unsigned long section_nr, s_begin, start_pfn;
>>> +	struct mem_section *ms;
>>> +	int nid;
>>> +
>>> +	clear_bit(PGDAT_KMIGRATED_ACTIVATE, &pgdat->flags);
>>> +	s_begin = next_present_section_nr(-1);
>>> +	for_each_present_section_nr(s_begin, section_nr) {
>>> +		start_pfn = section_nr_to_pfn(section_nr);
>>
>> I may be missing something, but in pghot_setup_hot_map() and
>> kmigrated_do_work() we seem to iterate over all memory sections. On large
>> memory systems, could this become a bottleneck right?
>>
> I think this is going to end up being a function of page / section size.
>
> I would hesitate to over-optimize on this early-on, otherwise we'll find

Sure.

> ourselves re-building a bunch of DAMON functionality that chunks memory
> into regions and such.
>
> But it is a concern.
>
>> Since hot_map is allocated only for lower-tier memory and the hotness
>> information is primarily used there, would it make sense to skip scanning
>> higher-tier sections?
>>
>> for_each_online_node(nid) {
>>      if (node_is_toptier(nid))
>>        continue;
>>
>>      start_pfn = node_start_pfn(nid);
>>      end_pfn = node_end_pfn(nid);
>>
>>      s_begin = pfn_to_section_nr(start_pfn);
>>      for_each_present_section_nr(s_begin, section_nr) {
>>    }
>> }
>>
>> Would this approach be reasonable, or am I overlooking something?
>>
> re: your note later on - kmigrated doesn't run for top tier nodes
>
> kmigrated_run() { if (node_is_toptier(nid)) return 0; }
>
>>> +
>>> +static int kmigrated_run(int nid)
>>> +{
>>> +	pg_data_t *pgdat = NODE_DATA(nid);
>>> +	int ret;
>>> +
>>> +	if (node_is_toptier(nid))
>>> +		return 0;
>> I might be missing something, but since this function is only called from
>> pghot_init(), would it make sense to check the condition before calling
>> kmigrated_run() to avoid the function call overhead?
>>
> this gets optimized out when static is inlined and optimized.
>
Thanks, Gregory.

I might be missing something, but since kmigrated_run() is not marked 
inline (static int kmigrated_run()), will it still be optimized or 
inlined by the compiler without the inline keyword?- Donet


>
> ~Gregory
>

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH RFC v3 01/19] mm: thread user_addr through page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch aims to thread user_addr through the page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Gregory agrees with David Hildenbrand's approach and is waiting for a new version of the patch (v4).

review_comments.author: Michael S. Tsirkin
review_comments.summary: Michael is expecting a new version of the patch (v4) and has acknowledged it.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC PATCH 00/12] dax: Add DAX to guest memfd support for KVM
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Proposal to add DAX support for guest memfd in KVM, allowing guests to use physical memory regions directly.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Identified potential benefits and drawbacks of using private nodes versus ZONE_DEVICE memory for guest memfd, suggesting that both approaches have their use cases. Expressed support for the idea but questioned its flexibility.

review_comments.author: Frank van der Linden
review_comments.summary: Shared similar concerns about using private nodes for guest memory, but suggested that both approaches could coexist with registered backend allocators. Expressed enthusiasm for the idea and its potential applications.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH RFC] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes

version_history.subject: [PATCH RFC] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes the SVC_POOL_* enum and mode selection heuristic, simplifying the pool map functions to always use the pernode path. The percpu mode is removed as it created excessive pools relative to the number of threads most deployments run. The module parameter and netlink interfaces are preserved for backward compatibility, but writing any value other than 'pernode' will return an error. The default pool mode is hardcoded to pernode, which provides one pool per node with proper thread affinity and NUMA-local memory allocation.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever expressed concern that the patch hard-fails on legacy module parameter settings, instead recommending acceptance and ignoring of old values for backwards compatibility. He suggested changing the 'auto' mode to per-node and adding deprecation warnings before removal of other modes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Generally, I think the end goal of making "per-node" the default
and only setting is correct. My comments below are about how we
get there.

The main concern is not perturbing any configuration that today
happens to set the module parameter.

The proposed patch correctly preserves the shape of the user/kernel
interfaces (same module parameter name and perms, same netlink command
and attribute, same exported symbol names and signatures). The concern
is that the accepted input set has narrowed from four strings to one
and the setters reject the legacy three with -EINVAL. For the module
parameter that path runs at module load, so existing modprobe.d configs
written any time in the last ~19 years will cause load-time parameter
errors. Some might categorize that as a regression.
                                      
The commit message documents that non-"pernode" writes now return
-EINVAL. The historically correct approach for this kind of obsoleted
tuning knob is to accept-and-ignore the old values (plus the pr_notice)
rather than hard-fail.

Or, put another way, the proposed patch implements something slightly
different than true backwards compatibility. Userspace that previously
set pool_mode=global/percpu/auto now gets -EINVAL, which technically
speaking is a behavioral narrowing, not "backward compatibility."

I might go even further and suggest that perhaps for v7.2:

- Change the behavior of "auto" to be per-node
- Add a deprecation warning that is emitted when setting other modes,
  but don't warn when the value is specifically the only accepted one.

Then wait a few more cycles before removal of percpu and global.

Other notes:

o The existing pernode path assumes every online node has both CPUs and
  some memory. nr_online_nodes on some platforms (e.g., certain ARM64,
  CXL) counts memoryless or CPU-less nodes; for_each_node_with_cpus()
  vs. for_each_online_node() matters here. See
  svc_pool_map_init_pernode().

o Recommend review of some history on this topic:
  https://lore.kernel.org/linux-nfs/f027319a-378d-4b91-a418-c45218fb7a21@oracle.com/


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Chuck Lever's concern about the default pool_mode being 'global' instead of 'auto', explaining that 'pernode' is functionally identical to 'global' on single-NUMA hosts and 'percpu' mode is useless. Author suggests changing the default to 'pernode' initially as a compromise.

Author agrees that a warning is necessary when removing the percpu option, indicating a fix will be included in future revisions.

Author acknowledges that removing all pool modes except pernode is a significant change and agrees to stage it in slowly, but expresses concern about following up with later phases of deprecation.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's already the case if you're on a NUMA box. The problem is that
the default is not "auto" but "global". We could change that (easily),
but I'd argue that "auto" mode just devolves into "pernode" for two
reasons:

- "pernode" is functionally identical to "global" when there is only a
single NUMA node

- "percpu" mode is basically useless

If we want to "go slow" on this, then what I'd probably suggest is that
we just change the default to "pernode" initially.

Single node boxes that have this set to global today should see no real
change (functionally identical). Boxes with multiple NUMA nodes that
don't set it now, will start using pernode mode, but that's probably a
good thing in most cases.

---

We probably do still want to warn in that case. Once we remove the
option, the module load can fail, so we probably want to discourage
people from keeping the setting.

---

That's fair. We can stage this in slowly.

I'm terrible about following up with the later phases of long term
deprecation though. Suggestions on how to make sure we don't drop the
ball on finishing the change?

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the distinction between 'auto' and 'default' pool modes is unclear, describing it as 'whacky'.

Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that setting a deprecated pool mode should not cause module loading to fail, as failing to load would be more painful than loading and having different thread affinity behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
OK, there seems to be a difference between "auto" and "default". That's
a little whacky.

---

I'm saying that setting a deprecated pool mode should not cause
module loading to fail at all. Failing to load will be more painful
than loading and having different thread affinity behavior.


-- 
Chuck Lever

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH RFC] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch removes the SVC_POOL_* enum and mode selection heuristic, simplifying the pool map functions to always use the pernode path. The percpu mode is removed as it created excessive pools relative to the number of threads most deployments run. The module parameter and netlink interfaces are preserved for backward compatibility, but writing any value other than 'pernode' will return an error. The default pool mode is hardcoded to pernode, which provides one pool per node with proper thread affinity and NUMA-local memory allocation.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the distinction between 'auto' and 'default' pool modes is unclear, describing it as 'whacky'.

Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that setting a deprecated pool mode should not cause module loading to fail, as failing to load would be more painful than loading and having different thread affinity behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
OK, there seems to be a difference between "auto" and "default". That's
a little whacky.

---

I'm saying that setting a deprecated pool mode should not cause
module loading to fail at all. Failing to load will be more painful
than loading and having different thread affinity behavior.


-- 
Chuck Lever

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v1 00/13] fuse: dir.c cleanups

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 01/13] fuse: fix missing forget requests in error paths
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module where forget requests were not being sent to the server when it returned valid node IDs but invalid attributes, leading to potential server-side leaks. The patch modifies error paths in the fuse_lookup_name and fuse_create_open functions to send forget requests when necessary. This ensures that the server releases its reference on the file, preventing memory leaks. Although the real-world impact is minimal due to the specific conditions required for this bug to occur, sending forget requests is still essential for maintaining a healthy connection with the FUSE server.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Amir Goldstein noted that the patch introduces a repeating pattern and suggested hiding it behind a helper function to validate expected attributes
review_comments.raw_body:
This seems like a repeating pattern.
Would you consider hiding this behind a helper that validates we got
an expected set of attr:

        if (fuse_invalid_attr_ftype(&outarg.attr, mode & S_IFMT))

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author agreed to add a new helper function as requested by the reviewer, indicating that the patch will need further revision.
review_comments.raw_body:
I will add this helper.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 02/13] fuse: clean up fuse_lookup_name()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the fuse_lookup_name() function by removing a goto statement and replacing it with direct returns. It also introduces a local variable to hold the value of fm->fc, making the code more readable and easier to maintain.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 03/13] fuse: clean up fuse_lookup()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the fuse_lookup() function by removing goto statements and direct returns, making the code more readable and maintainable without introducing any functional changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 04/13] fuse: clean up fuse_dentry_revalidate()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch cleans up the fuse_dentry_revalidate() function in the Linux kernel's fuse module by removing gotos and direct returns, and flattening a nested if/else structure. The changes do not introduce any functional differences but improve code readability and maintainability. The patch is part of a larger series of 13 patches that aim to clean up and modernize the fuse module.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned the usefulness of a local variable 'need_reval' due to it being used only once, and suggested its name does not clearly convey any necessary information.

Reviewer suggested moving a kfree call to before the spin_lock(&fi->lock); statement in fuse_dentry_revalidate() function, improving code cleanliness and reducing potential issues.

Reviewer noted that fuse_dentry_revalidate() can return early without acquiring the per-vswap spinlock, potentially causing a lock ordering violation when reclaim paths are executed.
review_comments.raw_body:
I am not sure that this help var is really useful?
It is used just once in the next line and its name does not clarify
something that needs to be clarified.

---

Nit: line spacing is weird.
I would do this kfree before the nlookup++ block.

---

Apart from the nits, feel free to add:
Reviewed-by: Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com>

Thanks,
Amir.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 05/13] fuse: clean up fuse_create_open()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the fuse_create_open() function to remove a goto statement and instead use direct returns for error conditions, making the code more straightforward and easier to read without introducing any functional changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 06/13] fuse: clean up fuse_rename2()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the fuse_rename2() function by moving flag validation into a separate branch and returning early to eliminate unnecessary code. The change does not introduce any functional changes, only refactoring the existing logic.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 07/13] fuse: clean up fuse_time_to_jiffies()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes an unnecessary else statement from the fuse_time_to_jiffies() function in the Linux kernel's fs/fuse/dir.c file, making the code cleaner without introducing any functional changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 08/13] fuse: clean up fuse_link()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the fuse_link() function in the Linux kernel's fs/fuse/dir.c file to remove a goto statement and replace it with a direct return, improving code cleanliness and maintainability. The patch also changes an -ENOSYS check to be an else if condition, making the code more readable. There are no functional changes as a result of this refactoring.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 09/13] fuse: clean up fuse_do_getattr()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch restructures the logic in the fuse_do_getattr function to reduce if-else nesting without making any functional changes. The code is refactored to improve readability and maintainability, but its behavior remains unchanged.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 10/13] fuse: clean up fuse_update_get_attr()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the logic in fuse_update_get_attr() by consolidating conditions for determining whether to sync and returning early from the sync path, reducing if-else nesting without introducing any functional changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 11/13] fuse: clean up fuse_get_link()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the fuse_get_link() function by replacing goto statements with direct returns. The function now directly returns error pointers instead of jumping to a label and returning an error pointer from there. This change does not introduce any functional changes, only improves code readability and maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 12/13] fuse: clean up fuse_dir_open()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch restructures the logic in the fuse_dir_open function to reduce if-else nesting without introducing any functional changes. The original code had a deeply nested if-else statement, which has been refactored into a more straightforward and maintainable structure.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 13/13] fuse: clean up setattr()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the fuse_do_setattr() and fuse_setattr() functions by using a cached file connection (fc) instead of retrieving it from the mount data each time. This change does not introduce any functional changes, only improving performance by reducing unnecessary lookups.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patchset is a series of cleanups in the fuse: dir.c file, addressing code structure issues pointed out by Amir. The changes aim to improve the maintainability and readability of the code without introducing any functional changes, except for the first patch which fixes missing forget requests in error paths.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about missing forget requests in error paths, explaining that these paths only trigger with misbehaving servers returning invalid attributes and that the real-world impact is minimal. The author agreed to send a forget request to tell the server to release its reference.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed the concern about using goto statements in fuse_lookup_name() by removing them and directly returning instead. The code was also simplified by assigning fm->fc to a local variable, fc, and using that throughout the function.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed the concern about gotos with direct returns in fuse_lookup() by removing them and simplifying the d_splice_alias error path, confirming that no functional changes were intended.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed Amir's concern about the code structure in dir.c, specifically fuse_dentry_revalidate(), by removing gotos and flattening nested if/else structures, confirming no functional changes are intended.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the use of goto statements in fuse_create_open() and has removed it, replacing it with a direct return statement.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the code structure in dir.c by moving flag validation into the flags branch and returning early to eliminate the else block, making no functional changes.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about unnecessary code in fuse_time_to_jiffies() by removing an else statement, confirming no functional changes were intended.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the use of goto in fuse_link() and agreed to restructure it by removing the goto statement and replacing it with an else if for -ENOSYS check, confirming no functional changes.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about if-else nesting in the code structure of fuse_dentry_revalidate(), agreed to restructure logic to reduce nesting, and provided an updated patch that includes these changes.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: Reviewer argued that the cleanup patches are not worth the effort due to potential backporting conflicts and excessive churn, requesting to drop all but the fuse_dentry_revalidate() patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think this is not a good idea.
These sort of cleanup (e.g. removing unneeded gotos) are a kin to
whitespace cleanups - we should not do them unless going to change
the function for another reason.

The churn this creates and backporting conflicts are not worth the
win of the cleanup itself.

Therefore, please drop all but the fuse_dentry_revalidate() patch
which should be part of your passthrough series.

I will review only this one patch.

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary: reviewer noticed a repeated pattern in fuse_dentry_revalidate() and suggested hiding it behind a helper function that validates the expected attributes
review_comments.raw_body:
This seems like a repeating pattern.
Would you consider hiding this behind a helper that validates we got
an expected set of attr:

        if (fuse_invalid_attr_ftype(&outarg.attr, mode & S_IFMT))

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Amir Goldstein
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned the usefulness of a local variable, suggesting it's only used once and its name doesn't provide clear information

Reviewer suggested that a kfree should be done before the nlookup++ block, noting that the current line spacing in the code is also weird

Reviewer noted that fuse_dentry_revalidate() has a code structure issue, but did not specify the exact problem or request any changes.
review_comments.raw_body:
I am not sure that this help var is really useful?
It is used just once in the next line and its name does not clarify
something that needs to be clarified.

---

Nit: line spacing is weird.
I would do this kfree before the nlookup++ block.

---

Apart from the nits, feel free to add:
Reviewed-by: Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com>

Thanks,
Amir.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a potential issue with backporting conflicts, decided to drop the current patchset and include only the fuse_dentry_revalidate() cleanup in a future passthrough series.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ah, I hadn't considered the backporting conflicts. That makes sense  -
I'll keep that in mind for the future.

I'll drop this series and include only the fuse_dentry_revalidate()
cleanup and have that as part of the passthrough series.

Thanks,
Joanne

I will add this helper.

Thanks,
Joanne

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v3] fuse: optional FORGET delivery over io_uring
Re: [PATCH] fuse: fix race between inode/dentry invalidation and readdir
Re: [PATCH v4 5/8] fuse: {io-uring} Allow reduced number of ring queues
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch proposes simplifying the logic for dispatching requests in the fuse io-uring implementation by using the group_cpus_evenly() function, which partitions CPUs based on numa nodes and other considerations. This would eliminate the need for per-numa state tracking and simplify queue selection to a simple cpu-to-qid lookup. Additionally, it suggests declaring the total queue count upfront and establishing the mapping at ring creation time, eliminating the rebuild-on-each-queue-creation complexity.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
Bernd Schubert expressed skepticism about the proposed change, questioning whether it's necessary and suggesting alternative priorities, such as addressing lock issues and enabling eBPF override of queue selection.

Reviewer Bernd Schubert disagreed that the current approach could make everything entirely dynamic, instead suggesting a config SQE would make sense and proposing an alternative where libfuse starts with minimal memory usage and dynamically adds ring entries based on request size
review_comments.raw_body:
Maybe, I need to check that code. However, does this really need to be
done right now? This cannot be updated later? For me it looks a bit like
we are going to replace one code by another, without a clear advantage.
I can look into group_cpus_evenly(), but I cannot promise you when that
will happen.
My personal preference would be to work on real issue, like getting rid
of two locks (queue->lock and bg->lock) and distribute max_bg accross
queues. And that probably requires the distribution across queues, which
you didn't like in the previous series. Anway, already finding the time
for that is hard.

My personal opinion is that queue selection needs to return the qid, so
that the function can be overriden with eBPF. I didn't have time yet to
try that out.

---

That is why I said in another mail that a config SQE would make to some
extend sense. However, the part where I disagree is that we could make
it all entirely dynamic with the current approach.
Only the logic for that in libfuse is missing. I.e. it _could_ start
with a single queue or one queue per numa and one ring entry. Basically
no memory usage then.
And now libfuse could add logic - many small requests - set up ring
entries with smaller payload size (or smaller pBuf). Many large requests
- add more requests with larger payload size. And with the current
approach queues can be added dynamically.


Thanks,
Bernd
